abstract: The proliferation of lithium microstructures during electrochemical cycling remains a critical barrier to the commercialization of high-energy-density Li-metal batteries. In this study, in situ $^7$Li magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) is employed to investigate the relationship between electrolyte concentration dynamics and the morphology of lithium deposition in symmetric cells. By utilizing chemical shift imaging (CSI), we distinguish between different deposition environments, specifically separating mossy lithium growth from runaway dendritic structures based on their unique bulk magnetic susceptibility signatures. Our results demonstrate that mossy structures begin to form almost immediately upon charging across all tested current densities. However, the transition to rapid dendritic growth occurs at a distinct onset time. At high current densities, this onset correlates strongly with the depletion of lithium ions near the electrode surface, consistent with the theoretical Sand’s time model. In contrast, at low current densities, dendrite initiation occurs significantly earlier than predicted by mass-transport limitations, suggesting that alternative mechanisms—such as those driven by surface inhomogeneities or solid electrolyte interphase (SEI) instability—dominate the low-current regime. These findings provide a robust experimental framework for quantifying the spatial and temporal evolution of lithium microstructures, offering insights essential for the development of strategies to mitigate dendrite formation.
